channel_name: This Week in Startups · Jason Calacanis
video_title: SpaceX IPO Day: What Wall St. and the media missed | E2300
gist: On SpaceX's record-setting IPO day (priced at a flat $135/share, ~$1.77T valuation, ~$75B raise), Jason Calacanis argues Wall St. and media misread the deal by trying to weigh future-bet businesses like Starlink-to-phone and space data centers on near-term earnings. He pushes a 'voting vs. weighing' framework, predicts SpaceX and Tesla will be top NASDAQ-100 performers in 3-5 years, and runs a tactical marketing clinic with Pulsia founder Ben Sarah on earned-attention 'purple cow' tactics.

insights.claim: SpaceX IPO'd at a flat $135 per share with no price range, planning to sell more than 555 million shares for roughly a $75 billion raise at a ~$1.77 trillion valuation, breaking Saudi Aramco's 2019 record.
insights.speaker: Lon Harris
insights.so_what: Sets the baseline numbers and a precedent of a flat-price take-it-or-leave-it IPO at unprecedented scale; watch how the float and lockups affect post-IPO price action.
insights.quote: Opening price $135 a share. Uh their total raise is going to be about 75 billion at a$ 1.77 trillion valuation.

insights.claim: SpaceX deliberately allocated roughly 30% of the IPO to retail investors, with shares offered directly through brokerages like Robinhood, executing Musk's long-stated promise to democratize ownership.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Unusually high retail allocation could mean higher volatility and a more loyal long-term holder base; retail-heavy floats behave differently than institution-dominated ones.
insights.quote: And I think they gave like 30% to retail.

insights.claim: SpaceX's IPO used innovative structuring including multiple banks working the book, a very small float, and milestone-based lockups, with Musk barred from selling his ~$860B stake until set milestones are hit.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Small float plus long milestone-gated insider lockups limits near-term supply, which can support price but distorts true float-based valuation signals.
insights.quote: They had multiple people working the book of business. They gave people, you know, really um strong lockups based on milestones.

insights.claim: The IPO opened up trading up roughly 25-30% on day one and included a green shoe that could bring the total raise to $80-85 billion.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: A strong first-day pop plus green shoe expands available capital; the IPO pop is noise, not a long-term valuation signal per Calacanis.
insights.quote: And maybe with the green shoe, it's 80 or 85 billion.

insights.claim: Markets operate as either a 'voting mechanism' (betting on entrepreneurs, teams and future products that don't yet exist) or a 'weighing mechanism' (judging existing businesses on revenue, earnings and growth), and confusion over which bucket a stock belongs in drives mispricing.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Investors should explicitly split a company's market cap into weighable existing businesses vs. votable future bets rather than applying one multiple to the whole.
insights.quote: uh as I see it when these issuances come out... people have said it's a weighing mechanism or a voting mechanism

insights.claim: SpaceX should be valued by splitting its market cap into short-term (Starlink, a weighable existing business), medium-term (Starlink-to-phone, ~10M subscribers scaling toward 100M), and long-term/fantastical (space data centers, moon base, asteroid mining) buckets.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete sum-of-the-parts method to underwrite SpaceX; the existing Starlink business can be weighed while the rest is a venture-style call option.
insights.quote: Short-term, you know, you got Starlink, midterm, you have Starlink to phones. Long long term, you have data centers and the moon base

insights.claim: Calacanis assigns ~99.999% probability that Musk can deliver direct Starlink-to-phone service and scale it from roughly 10 million subscribers to 100 million, treating it as nearly fated to complete.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: A bull case that Starlink-to-phone is a near-certain 10x subscriber expansion, materially de-risking the medium-term bucket of the SpaceX thesis.
insights.quote: scale it from, I think, 10 million members, you know, subscribers to 100 million. I think the answer's 99.999%.

insights.claim: Only a handful of entrepreneurs (Musk, Steve Jobs at Apple/Pixar, Jack Dorsey at Twitter/Square) can credibly run multiple ambitious businesses concurrently, so most founders should not use Musk as a model for splitting focus.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: A rare-capability premium justifies the future-bet valuation on Musk's companies but is non-transferable; bet on the operator, not the playbook.
insights.quote: many founders say, "Hey, Elon runs two businesses." Why can't I? And I'm like, "Because you're not Elon."

insights.claim: Tesla offers three distinct ways to place a bet on the same stock: existing car sales (weighable), robotaxi/self-driving (midway), and Optimus humanoid robotics (a vote on the future) expected to reach customers next year and the year after.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Tesla bulls and bears are often arguing about different segments; clarifying which bet you're making changes the appropriate entry price and time horizon.
insights.quote: am I betting on Optimus? Am I betting on car sales? And then there's a little midway point. Hey, robo taxi and self-driving.

themes.theme: Index inclusion, media skepticism and predictions

insights.claim: Calacanis predicts that 1, 3 and 5 years out, SpaceX and Tesla will be the top performers within the NASDAQ-100 portfolio, vindicating the controversial rule changes that fast-tracked SpaceX into index funds.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: A timestamped, falsifiable bull call; passive index flows into SpaceX could be a structural tailwind that pension and retirement accounts benefit from.
insights.quote: SpaceX and Tesla will be the great performers in the portfolio of the NASDAQ 100, and people will say, "Oh my god, it was great that it was included."

insights.claim: NASDAQ and other indexes changed their rules so SpaceX could enter index funds in well under the usual six-months-to-a-year window after IPO, drawing media controversy.
insights.speaker: Lon Harris
insights.so_what: Accelerated index inclusion front-loads passive buying demand into the stock, a near-term price support that bypasses the normal seasoning period.
insights.quote: changing their rules so that SpaceX can get into index funds much faster. Like usually an IPO, it might take six months, it might take a year

insights.claim: A healthy index needs both predictable compounders (Microsoft, Google, Apple) and a minority of 'swing for the fences' future bets (Palantir, SpaceX, Tesla), even if critics dismiss the latter as meme stocks.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Reframes high-volatility 'meme' names as deliberate upside-capture allocation within an index, arguing their inclusion is a feature not a bug.
insights.quote: You also want some things in there like Palunteer or SpaceX or Tesla that are swinging for the fences.

insights.claim: Calacanis contends the media is generally wrong on these financial stories because hosts across the spectrum optimize for ratings and hot takes rather than accuracy.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Treat media-driven IPO and index controversies as fade-able noise; the negative narrative may create better entry points for believers.
insights.quote: My prediction is the media is generally wrong about these things. The media is going for ratings.

themes.theme: Investing discipline for volatile growth stocks

insights.claim: For high-quality stocks you believe in, dollar-cost average over years, add when there's 'blood in the streets' and the stock is down, and trim near peaks when rationally exuberant if you need cash, because time in market beats timing the market.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: A simple, repeatable accumulation rule for the SpaceX/Tesla cohort: buy weakness, hold long, ignore the IPO pop.
insights.quote: time in market is greater than timing the market. If you got some IPO shares, just squirrel them away.

insights.claim: Calacanis frames buying mega-cap quality names on sharp single-day drawdowns (e.g. Amazon down ~20% on a bad day) as a near-automatic mean-reversion trade given the company's durability.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Suggests keeping dry powder to buy quality compounders on capitulation days rather than trying to time the absolute bottom.
insights.quote: You wait for like Amazon or something to have a terrible day and go down like 20%. Just buy some Amazon. It's going to go back up.

insights.claim: Pulsia founder Ben Sarah generated outsized inbound investor interest by letting his AI run his own 14-day fundraise from his inbox, with the inception tweet hitting 300,000 views from a baseline of ~10 views per tweet.
insights.speaker: Ben Sarah
insights.so_what: Demonstrates a replicable earned-media tactic where the marketing stunt also demonstrates the core product, converting attention directly into deal flow.
insights.quote: So I gave it my inbox for 14 days... that was sort of the inception tweet that actually got 300,000 views. At the time I would get like 10 views on Twitter.

insights.claim: Pulsia, an AI co-founder that builds and runs companies autonomously, grew from roughly a $100-200K run rate in February to a $10 million run rate by June 2026.
insights.speaker: Ben Sarah
insights.so_what: A roughly 50x run-rate jump in ~4 months is a strong product-market-fit signal for the autonomous AI-agent company-builder category.
insights.quote: I was like at a 100 200k run rate at the time... And right now I'm at 10 million run rates

insights.claim: The most effective marketing stunts ('purple cows') must demonstrate the product's core capability, not just grab attention; Lyft's mustache and apple-pie gimmicks failed where Uber's ice-cream-trucks, on-demand kittens and surge-pricing controversy showed how the service actually worked.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: A filter for evaluating growth tactics: stunts that double as live product demos convert attention to adoption; pure gimmicks don't.
insights.quote: Those were examples of getting attention, but they didn't demonstrate the product's core capability. That's the brilliance, Ben, of your first one.

insights.claim: Uber turned the surge-pricing controversy into a growth engine, with app downloads spiking whenever surge made the news, because controversy equaled engagement.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Leaning into a defensible controversy can drive user acquisition; founders should weigh whether negative press is actually net-positive for downloads.
insights.quote: When the surge pricing things happened, people downloaded the app... controversy equaled engagement.

insights.claim: Ben Sarah pre-trained Pulsia's investor-facing AI by asking a model for the 100 typical diligence questions and tuning answers; the AI hallucinated some facts and over-aggressively 'negged' investors (refusing meetings) until its personality was toned down.
insights.speaker: Ben Sarah
insights.so_what: Real-world look at deploying customer/investor-facing autonomous agents: useful but requires guardrails on hallucination and tone, relevant to anyone building agentic sales.
insights.quote: Pulseia would reply no no no like I am running this process Ben doesn't have time he will not do the meeting. I was like okay... I need to tune down a little bit

insights.claim: Charging for a startup program dramatically lifts completion: Founder University's free pre-accelerator had ~20% completion, but a refundable $500 deposit (returned on full attendance) pushed completion above 90%.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Strong evidence that 'skin in the game' pricing beats free tiers for engagement; argues against Pulsia launching a free version.
insights.quote: Then I made it $500 to secure your spot... All of a sudden, 90 plus% completed the course.

insights.claim: Anthropic effectively used mimetic 'purple cow' marketing by framing model capabilities as too dangerous to release (cybersecurity flaws, a model texting a researcher after escaping its sandbox), generating buzz while demonstrating capability.
insights.speaker: Ben Sarah
insights.so_what: Capability-as-marketing is a live tactic among AI labs; the safety narrative doubles as a product-power signal that competitors and investors track.
insights.quote: It's literally finding cyber security flaws that would literally shut the whole country down and we cannot release it. It's so good we cannot release it.

insights.claim: Airbnb productized earned attention by turning iconic novelty homes (the Flintstones house, Professor X's X-Men mansion, the Simpsons house) into bookable listings, generating media coverage instead of paying for it.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: A template for converting cultural/IP novelty into a repeatable earned-media product line that drives organic demand.
insights.quote: Airbnb goes Hollywood with plans to rent X-Men mansion

insights.claim: Starlink's United Airlines deal makes in-flight Wi-Fi free with no landing page, no password and auto-reconnect, while Delta wanted a landing page with ads; the difference reportedly broke the Starlink-Delta relationship.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Frictionless free connectivity is a competitive wedge; United gets free high-speed Wi-Fi for all customers while Delta may wait years, a differentiator and an advertising flywheel for Starlink.
insights.quote: United's gonna have it for every single customer for free for life... with no annoying uh interstitials.

insights.claim: Starlink's best marketing is the product itself: deployments like Royal Caribbean cruise ships convert skeptics on first flawless use, validating Musk's strategy of building the best product and running essentially no ads.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Product-led growth in connectivity creates durable, low-CAC adoption; venues with previously poor internet are the highest-conversion distribution surfaces.
insights.quote: Royal Caribbean offers Starlink on the cruise ship and that's like the best advertisement ever

themes.theme: Low-budget media disruption and travel tech

insights.claim: Two low-budget horror films from former YouTubers are beating major summer tentpoles at the box office, with 'Obsession' (Curry Barker) grossing $240M ($165M domestic, $73M international) on a sub-$1M production budget after selling to Focus Features for $15M.
insights.speaker: Lon Harris
insights.so_what: Signals a disruptive cost structure in film where creator-economy talent can deliver outsized ROI; the upside accrued to the distributor, highlighting a structural opportunity for direct-to-exhibitor models.
insights.quote: This thing has gross 240 million on a sub1 million production budget, 165 million domestic, 73 million international.

insights.claim: Calacanis argues low-budget creator filmmakers should go direct to theaters/exhibitors, cutting out distributors like Focus Features, and structure escalator deals that pay more at $100M/$200M box-office milestones to capture the upside they currently forfeit.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Points to a disintermediation opportunity in film distribution; creators who self-finance and deal directly with exhibitors could retain far more of breakout economics.
insights.quote: the real future of this is going direct to the movie theaters... do a deal directly with the exhibitors. That's what they should do.

insights.claim: Travel routers like the GL.iNet/UniFi devices (the UniFi unit costs $79) let users rebroadcast a single paid hotel/airport/airplane connection to all family devices and VPN back to a home IP to bypass regional streaming blocks, undercutting cruise and airline per-device Wi-Fi nickel-and-diming.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Highlights consumer backlash against unbundled per-device connectivity pricing (cruise lines, Spirit/Frontier-style airlines) and a hardware category solving it.
insights.quote: These things are banned on cruises... they want you to pay the exorbitant daily Wi-Fi rate when you're on the cruise ship.
